content.label: Essential Question
content.question:
Should We Break Up Big Tech?

Arguments FOR: Too much market power, stifle competition, privacy concerns, prices higher than with competition

Arguments AGAINST: Services are free, innovation benefits users, breaking up reduces efficiency, successful companies shouldn't be punished

Today you'll learn WHEN government intervention makes economic sense—and when it doesn't!

objectives.description: market failures that justify government intervention: monopoly power, externalities, public goods, and information asymmetry
the economic roles of government including property rights enforcement, public goods provision, and market regulation
how taxes, subsidies, and price controls affect market outcomes and consumer/producer welfare
trade-offs between government intervention and market freedom using cost-benefit analysis

terms.definition: When free markets don't allocate resources efficiently, leading to suboptimal outcomes
terms.example: Pollution from factories (costs not reflected in price)

terms.definition: Cost or benefit affecting third parties who aren't involved in the transaction
Good that is non-excludable (can't prevent use) and non-rivalrous (one person's use doesn't reduce availability)
terms.example: National defense protects everyone, can't charge individuals

terms.definition: When one or few sellers control a market, restricting output and raising prices above competitive levels
terms.example: Your electric company—only one provider in your area

terms.definition: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other, leading to market inefficiency
terms.example: Used car seller knows problems, buyer doesn't (lemons problem)

terms.definition: Government payment to reduce costs or encourage production of specific goods
terms.example: Farm subsidies for corn, soybean production

layoutData.title: When Free Markets Fail: Five Key Problems
paragraphs: Market failure occurs when free markets don't allocate resources efficiently. Government intervention may be justified when these failures occur:
bulletPoints: 1. Monopoly Power: One seller restricts output, raises prices (utilities, Big Tech)
2. Negative Externalities: Third parties harmed, costs ignored (pollution, traffic)
3. Positive Externalities: Third parties benefit, under-produced (education, vaccines)
4. Public Goods: Free-rider problem, underprovided (national defense, parks)
5. Information Asymmetry: One party knows more than other (used cars, insurance)
layoutData.keyPoint: Not all market failures justify intervention—must weigh costs vs. benefits of government action

leftPanel.title: The Problem
paragraphs: Example: Your electric company—only one provider, you pay their rate with no alternatives!

highlightBox.text: Without competition, monopolies have no incentive to lower prices or improve quality

infoCard.title: Government Solutions
items: ✂️ Antitrust Enforcement: Break up monopolies (Standard Oil 1911, AT&T 1982)
📋 Regulate Natural Monopolies: Set maximum prices for utilities where competition is impossible

leftColumn.title: Negative Externalities
items: Solution: TAX the activity (carbon tax, cigarette tax, gas tax)

rightColumn.title: Positive Externalities
items: • Education benefits society beyond the individual
• R&D benefits extend beyond the inventor
Problem: Individual doesn't capture all benefits → Underproduction
Solution: SUBSIDIZE the activity (free K-12, vaccine programs)

leftPanel.title: Two Key Characteristics
paragraphs: 1. Non-excludable: Can't prevent people from using it
2. Non-rivalrous: One person's use doesn't reduce availability for others
🌲 Public parks are open to everyone

highlightBox.text: The Free-Rider Problem: People benefit without paying, so private companies won't provide

infoCard.title: Government Solution
items: Without government provision, these goods would be severely underprovided or not provided at all

layoutData.title: When one party knows more than the other in a transaction
paragraphs: Seller knows problems, buyers assume all are 'lemons,' good cars exit market
You know your health, sick people buy while healthy skip, leading to market collapse
Sellers know risks, buyers often don't—2008 financial crisis example
bulletPoints: Quality Standards: FDA drug approval, safety inspections
Licensing: Doctors, lawyers, contractors must meet standards
Consumer Protection Laws: Lemon laws, return policies
layoutData.keyPoint: Information asymmetry can cause markets to collapse entirely—government can restore function through disclosure and standards

layoutData.title: Beyond market failure correction, government provides essential economic functions
bulletPoints: ⚖️ 1. Enforce Property Rights: Courts, police, legal system that enables market transactions
🛡️ 2. Provide Public Goods: Defense, parks, street lights that markets won't provide
🌫️ 3. Correct Externalities: Tax pollution, subsidize education to align private/social costs
📋 4. Regulate Markets: Antitrust, safety standards, disclosure requirements
📊 5. Stabilize Economy: Fiscal and monetary policy to reduce business cycles (L-53)
💵 6. Redistribute Income: Progressive taxes and safety nets to reduce inequality
layoutData.keyPoint: Even supporters of free markets recognize these roles as necessary for markets to function properly

content.question:
Big Tech Regulation: Should government break up Google, Facebook, and Amazon?

Consider: Is there evidence of monopoly power? Are consumers being harmed? What are the costs and benefits of breaking them up? Are there better alternatives to antitrust action?

content.headerTitle: Real-World Example: How Taxes Affect Markets

scenario.name: The Gasoline Tax Story
paragraphs: Supply curve shifts LEFT (up)—sellers receive less, buyers pay more
Both consumers and producers share the tax burden
Total: ~$0.30-0.60/gallon added to the price you pay

outcomes.label: Purpose
outcomes.detail: 1) Raise revenue for roads, 2) Discourage driving (negative externality)

outcomes.label: Trade-off
outcomes.detail: Is the tax high enough to change behavior but not so high it unfairly burdens rural drivers?

content.headerTitle: Real-World Example: How Subsidies Affect Markets

scenario.name: Agricultural Subsidies in Action
paragraphs: Supply curve shifts RIGHT (down)—lower costs for producers
U.S. government pays billions in farm subsidies for corn, soybeans, and wheat
Benefits: Food security, lower food prices, rural support

outcomes.label: Unintended Effect
outcomes.detail: Led to high-fructose corn syrup in everything, contributing to obesity epidemic

outcomes.label: Trade-off
outcomes.detail: Do subsidies achieve food security goals or just benefit large agribusiness?

content.question:
Minimum Wage Debate: Should the federal minimum wage be raised to $15/hour?

Think about: Who benefits from higher minimum wage? Who might be harmed? What trade-offs exist? Is there a 'right' level, or does it depend on local cost of living?

leftColumn.title: Price Ceiling (Maximum Price)
items: Winners: Those who get rent-controlled apartments

rightColumn.title: Price Floor (Minimum Price)
items: ✓ Workers who keep jobs earn more
Losers: Some workers lose jobs (magnitude debated)

questions.question: What are the five types of market failure that can justify government intervention?
Explain the difference between negative and positive externalities. Give an example of each and describe the appropriate government response.
What is the free-rider problem and why does it lead to underprovision of public goods?
Compare the effects of taxes and subsidies on supply curves, prices, and quantities in a market.

takeaways.title: Five market failures justify intervention
takeaways.description: Monopoly power, negative/positive externalities, public goods, and information asymmetry create inefficient outcomes

takeaways.title: Government has six economic roles
takeaways.description: Property rights, public goods provision, externality correction, market regulation, economic stabilization, income redistribution

takeaways.title: Taxes shift supply LEFT
takeaways.description: Leading to higher prices and lower quantities—used to discourage negative externalities or raise revenue

takeaways.title: Subsidies shift supply RIGHT
takeaways.description: Leading to lower prices and higher quantities—used to encourage positive externalities

takeaways.title: Price controls create problems
takeaways.description: Ceilings cause shortages and black markets; floors cause surpluses and unemployment

takeaways.title: Every policy has trade-offs
takeaways.description: Benefits vs. costs, winners vs. losers—smart citizens evaluate whether intervention improves outcomes

main.title: Tomorrow's Learning Lab
main.description: Apply your knowledge by analyzing real government policies and their economic impact

steps: Analyze state policies and their impact on YOUR finances
Evaluate specific regulations: minimum wage, environmental rules, licensing requirements
Conduct cost-benefit analysis of proposed policies
Develop informed opinions on government's proper economic role
Use the Government Services Analyzer tool to assess local policies

content.tagline: "Understanding when markets need government—and when they don't"
